privilege_notes: runs fetched package code at launch

findings.title: Installed on-the-fly via a package runner
findings.detail: Server 'chrome-devtools' is fetched and executed at launch via 'npx' with auto-accept flag(s) -y; package spec: chrome-devtools-mcp.
findings.recommendation: Install the server as a pinned dependency and invoke the local binary, or vendor it. If a runner is unavoidable, pin an exact version (see unpinned_source).

findings.title: Package source is unpinned or mutable
findings.detail: Server 'chrome-devtools' package spec 'chrome-devtools-mcp' is unpinned (pin strength: none): no version specified (resolves to whatever the registry serves now).
findings.recommendation: Pin an exact version (pkg@1.2.3) or a git commit SHA. Avoid @latest and branch refs.

tools.name: fill_form
tools.description: Fill out multiple form elements (inputs, selects, checkboxes, radios) at once. ALWAYS prefer this tool over multiple individual 'fill' or 'click' calls when interacting with forms. tools.name: take_snapshot
tools.description:
Take a text snapshot of the currently selected page based on the a11y tree. The snapshot lists page elements along with a unique
identifier (uid). Always use the latest snapshot. Prefer taking a snapshot over taking a screenshot. The snapshot indicates the element selected
in the DevTools Elements panel (if any).

tools.name: wait_for
tools.description: Wait for the specified text to appear on the selected page.

toxic_combinations.title: Read-then-send exfiltration chain
toxic_combinations.detail: The server can both access secrets/credentials and make outbound network calls — a path to exfiltrate them to an attacker-chosen host.

toxic_combinations.title: Filesystem mutation driven by network input
toxic_combinations.detail: The server can write/delete files and make network calls — remote input can drive destructive or persistence-establishing writes.

toxic_combinations.title: Arbitrary file read paired with egress
toxic_combinations.detail: The server can read file/message contents and make outbound network calls — a read-then-send exfiltration path. The read accepts an arbitrary path (or the server is scoped to a broad directory), so it can reach secrets/configs far beyond the task.
